headline: Functionland Bets on AI Sovereignty as FULA Network Grows
subtitle: With 31 active nodes and a privacy-first MCP integration, Functionland positions itself at the intersection of decentralized storage and the AI data-control movement.
briefing: Functionland's FULA Network ticked up to 31 active nodes this week — a modest but steady gain visible in real time at cloud.fx.land/stats — as the project continues building out the community-contributed infrastructure it will need to compete in a rapidly maturing decentralized storage market. The more strategically significant development, however, may be the FxFiles MCP feature unveiled earlier this month. By allowing users to grant an AI assistant access to a single shared folder — with read and write permissions but no delete capability — Functionland is directly addressing a concern that is becoming central to enterprise AI adoption. The Model Context Protocol, originally open-sourced by Anthropic in November 2024, has seen explosive uptake: <cite index="27-5">MCP SDK downloads reached 97 million per month by March 2026, with over 10,000 public servers indexed across major registries.</cite> Yet that growth has exposed real risks; <cite index="23-4">Bloomberry research found that 38% of MCP servers have no authentication.</cite> Functionland's read/write-but-no-delete design principle maps directly onto the security principle of least-privilege access — exactly what regulators and security researchers are demanding. The macro tailwind is substantial: <cite index="4-11,4-13">venture capital funds invested more than $740 million in DePIN projects during 2025, with some estimates suggesting the sector could reach a potential valuation of $3.5 trillion by 2028.</cite> With community integrations expanding and node count climbing, the next signal to watch is whether Functionland can translate infrastructure growth into measurable paid storage usage — the metric that now separates credible DePIN projects from speculative ones.
